beats.text: You read picture graphs and bar graphs and used them to answer questions about the data. Now let's connect some ideas.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears on left. Favorite Sports data. Soccer=6, Basketball=4, Baseball=3, Tennis=2. Key: each symbol = 1 vote.
Horizontal bar graph appears on right. Same Favorite Sports data. Soccer=6, Basketball=4, Baseball=3, Tennis=2. Vertical axis labeled 0-7.

beats.text: Look at these two graphs. They show the SAME data.

options: Soccer has the most in both
beats.text: What's the same about both graphs? Select all that apply.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Favorite Pets data. Dogs=5, Cats=4, Fish=3, Birds=6. Three components labeled by system overlay: A=Title 'Favorite Pets', B=Key 'Each ★ = 1 vote', C=Category labels (Dogs, Cats, Fish, Birds). Labels are clickable.

beats.text: Every part of a graph has a job. This picture graph has three parts labeled A, B, and C. Click on the part that shows what each symbol means.
Which part tells you what each symbol means?

validator.description: Student clicked component B — the key — correct

params.description: Component B (the key) highlights with glow effect

beats.text: The key tells you what each symbol means. Here, each star equals 1 vote. Title tells what the graph is about. Labels name the categories. Key shows the scale. Every part has a job.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Favorite Pets data. Three labeled components visible: A=Title, B=Key, C=Category labels. click_component tool active.

beats.text: Graphs aren't just in math class. People use them everywhere. Look at these three scenarios. Which of these could use a bar graph to show the data? Select all that apply.
Which of these could use a bar graph to show the data? Select all that apply.
options: Your class votes on a field trip destination
Library tracks which book genre students check out most
Cafeteria tracks which lunch is most popular

and.selected: Your class votes on a field trip destination
Library tracks which book genre students check out most
Cafeteria tracks which lunch is most popular

validator.description: Student selected all three scenarios, correct

beats.text: Exactly. All of them! Any time you're counting and comparing categories, a graph can help.

or.selected: Your class votes on a field trip destination
Library tracks which book genre students check out most
Cafeteria tracks which lunch is most popular

and.selected: Your class votes on a field trip destination
Library tracks which book genre students check out most
Cafeteria tracks which lunch is most popular

validator.description: Branch 2: Student selected only correct answers but not all three

beats.text: You're on the right track. Each one you picked could use a bar graph. Look at the scenarios again. Are there any others that count and compare categories?

or.selected: Your class votes on a field trip destination
Library tracks which book genre students check out most
Cafeteria tracks which lunch is most popular

validator.description: Branch 3: Student selected no correct answers

beats.text: Let's think about what bar graphs show. They count things in different categories so you can compare them. Which scenarios count and compare?

or.selected: Your class votes on a field trip destination
Library tracks which book genre students check out most
Cafeteria tracks which lunch is most popular

validator.description: Branch 4: Student selected mix of correct and incorrect answers

beats.text: Some of your picks are right. Those scenarios do count and compare categories. Check your other selections. Do they count things in categories?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

beats.text: Let me show you how this works. Bar graphs count things in different categories. Field trips: you count votes for each destination. Library books: you count checkouts for each genre. Cafeteria: you count how many students chose each lunch. All three count and compare, so all three could use a bar graph. The answer is all of them.
Graphs help people see patterns and make decisions. You'll see them in school, in stores, on the news, everywhere.
You saw two kinds of comparison questions today. Some asked "how many more" and others asked "how many fewer." To answer comparisonquestions, what do you need to do?
To answer comparison questions like "how many more" and "how many fewer", what do you need to do?

validator.description: Student answered subtract — correct, both 'how many more' and 'how many fewer' require subtraction

beats.text: "How many more" and "how many fewer" both require subtraction. That's called COMPARING. You find the difference to compare. And remember: "in all" means COMBINE, so you add. {Compare} and {combine}, those are your two problem types.
Also remember: "in all" means COMBINE, so you add. {Compare} and {combine}, those are your two problem types.

params.description: Student's Warmup graph reappears on left — horizontal picture graph with 3 categories they created earlier
A Lesson graph from earlier sections appears on right — horizontal picture graph showing data they read during the lesson

beats.text: You made your own graph. You read graphs showing other people's data. You answered comparison questions and found totals. You know how to collect and represent data.
Next time, you'll see graphs where each symbol means more than one. Get ready for a new challenge.
